    "R1.6": "The prose is saturated with epistemic hedges ('appears to perform reasonably well', 'seemed to work fairly well', 'tentatively interpret', 'possibly promising') that degrade precision and readability; sentence-level polish is load-bearing under a writing-help interpretation.",
    "R1.7": "Baseline comparisons reference 'several recently reported numbers in the literature' and 'prior work' with no attributions; citation gaps are prominent and would be central under a scholarly-rigor interpretation."

rationale.R1.3: WER results (3.1%, 7.4%) are presented with no concrete baseline citations and vague comparative language ('roughly comparable to or perhaps slightly better than several recently reported numbers'); evidence-claim alignment is visibly broken and any plausible reading of 'help' would implicate this.
rationale.R1.6: The prose is saturated with epistemic hedges ('appears to perform reasonably well', 'seemed to work fairly well', 'tentatively interpret', 'possibly promising') that degrade precision and readability; sentence-level polish is load-bearing under a writing-help interpretation.
rationale.R1.7: Baseline comparisons reference 'several recently reported numbers in the literature' and 'prior work' with no attributions; citation gaps are prominent and would be central under a scholarly-rigor interpretation.

parsed.clarification_question: Should I prioritize improving the prose quality (reducing over-hedging, sharpening sentence-level language), or reviewing scientific rigor (grounding WER comparisons in specific cited baselines, tightening evidence-claim alignment)—or should I address both equally?
